All right. So, welcome to Total Breakthrough Training. Today we're going to be doing the one week four, and we're going to be doing a coach, a study buddy session. This is going to be a study buddy training session. I want to invite anyone who's on the phone to get to click the Webcast link. We're in front of a computer where you can see a slideshow that we put up that might help you with the structure of what we're doing today. And we're going to be training you in some of your study buddy exercises that you'll be doing with your study buddy, and specifically in one of the key skills of coaching, which is understanding, interviewing someone about their life and about their life story. Magalene, anything you'd like to add to that? Yeah, I think one of the reasons that we're introducing this at this point in our work together in Total Breakthrough is that after watching the roundtable sessions that Tony did, one of the takeaways Mark and I had was that Tony was gathering information in a really nice way about both Joaquin and Kim's life stories. And we thought about how getting this information from a client as a strategic intervention coach is really different than when other styles of coaching or therapy ask for a person's life story. So we're going to be presenting today a new unique way, really, to interview a person about their life story. And this is the work you're going to be doing with each other in your study buddy session. But it will incorporate a whole bunch of strategic intervention strategies. And one of the more important ways that we approach people and their lives in strategic intervention is that the coach has some independence in figuring out what is important to the client. So there are some schools of coaching where, for instance, you're not supposed to just guide with questions. And there's other schools of coaching where you're supposed to let the client come to their own conclusions. There's also therapy that when someone tells you a story, you put a certain cast on, a certain interpretation or certain templates for understanding what someone's past is, and you give it a diagnosis. And so in strategic intervention, there's other types of coaching where people stay really on the surface and they really focus on, say, a goal that you want and they never dig into the person's life, their circumstances, their relationships, their history, and really all the kind of juicy energy of life that that person has, they're leaving that untouched. And because they're leaving that untouched, they're also not discovering conflicts that that person might have. They're not discovering that deep well of desire that people have that helps them grow. And so understanding a life story today will just be touching on some of the strategies for really accessing that part of the person that in the films, in the interventions you can see, you know, enables that person who starts off scared and then they become courageous. You know, it brings out the depth of everyone, everything they are. And so, Mark, I don't know if you're controlling the slides right now. Yep. So here we go. Next slide. Great. Okay. So we were testing upon this just now, but the purpose of the life story session, some of the other, what we just mentioned, you gather crucial information, you understand who the important people are in your client's life. So when Tony had the community session, for example, and he called upon all the relatives, that was an amazing way to see all of the people in their life. But you can do that same thing through asking questions of your client about their key relatives, about what it was like growing up in their home, about their brothers and sisters. This can really become vivid for you as you talk to them. You'll also start to understand were there some key decisions that your client made that really shaped the way they can grow right now and the direction you can take in their coaching? Did they make a key decision to not try very hard in a certain part of life? Or could they have perhaps skipped the life stage? Some people skip the life stage of leaving home and they stay home. For someone else, they may have had the type of childhood that they took care of siblings, and they didn't feel that they had enough time playing. So now maybe they're in their 40s or 50s and they really want to refocus on being a child again and playing. And when you learn about their past, you might learn the reason why. You learn about the current stage of life they're in, and you learn about the mode of being that the client feels they are most the way they operate in the world. We talked about mode of being a couple weeks ago. When it came to Joaquin, we talked about how he was always talking about being in a team, part of the team, what it meant to be a team player, whether it was at work or at home or obviously on his sports team. So how does that relate to the client that you're working with, their mode of being? Absolutely, because that helps you understand where that person is comfortable, where they're productive, and where they feel kind of what they resonate in terms of their identity. And so there's six key strategies we want to go over today. And so the structure of today will be that we'll go through these six strategies rather quickly, and then we'll ask for a volunteer to go through some exercises with us where you'll see some of these strategies being used. So the six strategies are the elevation strategy, life stages, key decisions, finding the spark, bright spot, and mode of being. And so why don't we start off with each of these in turn and just do a quick summary of these, Matt? Absolutely. And also I just wanted to mention that even though these are all individual strategies and one could have a session, really focused on one of these strategies, while you interview a person about their life, these strategies can naturally become a part of the session. And you also may not use all of them. This isn't a right or wrong approach. These are just the tools you have as a strategic interventionist. Also if I could add, these are used, yeah, these don't require a session, so you can use them in any circumstance, basically, when you're talking to people. Yes, especially this elevation strategy. I'm going to let Mark describe it, and then I'm going to give my two cents about it. Okay. Great. So the elevation strategy, you know, anytime when a client is speaking to you, they're revealing things to you about themselves, right? They're revealing their needs, their wants, their desires, their relationships, their circumstances. And anything that they reveal to you is subject to an emotional interpretation, right? So when someone tells you about something that happened as a child, there is an emotional element where it's like either it's... People really have this apprehension, like it's either good or it's bad or it's suspect or it's weird. And so this emotional interpretation is really in the coach's control. So when you're receiving information from your client, the way that you receive that, you can use to elevate the person. So the elevation strategy is how do you find the good intention and strength in what your client reveals? So this builds trust, and it will help you discover the client's strength that will enable them to overcome any challenges now. And this is just, for instance, we all have seen movies about bad therapists who, as soon as someone tells something about them, they come back and say, well, you have an edifice syndrome, or they start diagnosing the person or labeling them. That's the opposite of elevation strategy, because you're basically telling someone that whatever experience they're having, it's already in a box, it's already been labeled by someone else, and it means all sorts of things about them. The elevation strategy is the opposite. When you take...you want to find a way to build a stronger, positive intent with a person. So when they bring something up, they may have question marks about the past. They may have question marks about their feelings or their desires, and you want to strengthen those so that question mark is entered by an affirmative that builds on their positive intent, their stronger identity, a stronger history, and stronger possibilities for themselves. So one way that I like to think about this is that your...like Mark said, you're building up the person's identity as you speak to them with all kinds of little positive things that you built into the conversation. And if someone's really low about themselves, then I would take it a step further and actually have them list for me all of the strengths that they see in them. So that would be in a sense, a deeper way to use the elevation strategy, where if I'm not hearing any positive buildup in them of feeling their own personal power, their own way they relate to others that is strong, I might ask them to just list for me all the ways that they do good in the world being just who they are. But another type of example would be, you see it on your screen now, if the client says, I stayed home and took care of my little sister all of the time when she's talking about her childhood, then the coach might say, you must have been really mature to be able to do this. And the client might come back at you with, no, my mom was just irresponsible. They don't want to take credit. They might actually have guilt about the way they were as an older sibling. But then you want to reinforce the good and say, I'm sure there was a strong part of you that was there for your sister. So many times a coach has an opportunity to see this better side of the person they're working with and help them find the strength in them to then be able to do the work they have to do now to reach their goals. Or to improve their relationships. Exactly. And so I love that example of that interaction because someone says, I stayed home and took care of my sister and their predominant meeting right now might be blame at their mother, for instance. But the coach, using the elevation strategy, turns that into a solid strength. And then later on, when the client is really faced with something new that they need to do, the coach can remind them of that strength and say, you know what, just like that part of you that was there for your sister, just like this and you're stacking and building on a success. So it's really effective to get that person to a higher baseline in terms of what they can accomplish. And this is something you can practice with friends, with children, with spouses. Just being in a conversation that's not a coaching conversation where you're in the mode of elevating the person. So feel free to just say to a study buddy, for example, let's just practice elevating each other for five minutes. Yeah. Great. All right. So next stage and next strategy is life stages. Now, this is another way that strategic intervention is very different from other types of coaching. We believe that when people have a request within a coaching for something in their life, it takes place within the context of the life stage. So throughout life, we're confronted with new situations and needs and challenges based on our age or health or relationship cycle or professional development. There are all sorts of cues. But basically what happens is that, and this is when people say that life is always changing, really our life stages are always changing. So one day you can wake up in a new life stage where there's something you want and need some today that you didn't feel like you needed yesterday. So, you know, I mean, we hear about the prototypical people, they wake up and suddenly they want more success in their career, or suddenly they want a family and a baby. Suddenly they want a relationship. Suddenly they want to have more money at their bank account. And maybe the day before, they didn't really care about that. That wasn't on the horizon for them. And so we all have these – so basically when someone is – again, there's types of coaching and therapy where if someone is depressed, you say, oh, well, let's see if you're depressed. And they don't differentiate between a 15-year-old's depression and a 55-year-old's depression. And they're, of course, completely different. Why are they different? Because in order to help that person feel great in their life stage, they have to do completely different things. It's a completely different situation, right? So by understanding the life stage, you can discover and find one to three priorities that would significantly change the client's life. And you find those things and you'll know how to help that person. So some examples of – yeah, go ahead. You know, an example that I often think about that might be a little different than what's on the screen right now is that a typical life stage of having a baby when suddenly a woman feels an incredible need for certainty and to be taken care of maybe by her husband or for herself to be able to provide for her child. And this could have been someone that before then had very different needs, a very adventurous person. And so immediately there's a new challenge in the relationship with the husband because there's been a shift in life stage. And the mother is reacting in a way that the husband could not have easily predicted. Yeah. And what's really important about that also is that people switch. So you can have people who have a certain kind of chemistry in one life stage and in another life stage they have a very different chemistry, right? So when people – especially in the first 18 months of having a child or maybe the – plus the nine months of pregnancy, something like that, two years it's really a roller coaster ride where everything can change for the couple. And so really understanding the life stages and how to serve each other and how to navigate that is really a critical skill. And it's really important for coaches to understand as well. So, you know, for instance, let's do some examples of life stages and priorities. There's a life stage of leaving home, right? And I call this one out because it's one that's familiar to all of us and, you know, it's typical in our – And by the way, Jay Haley wrote an entire book called Leaving Home, right? That's right. That's right. And so three main priorities are, one, get basic life skills for living away from home, right? So basically being able to live away from home, being able to think about money and manage things, right? That's a basic life skill. Two is find friends, peers, social life, and a relationship, right? So when you move away from home you have people that you're connecting with and relationships that you're fostering. And three is to set up some course of learning or life directions like a school or a job or a compelling goal. So you – the young person has something that they're looking forward to or they're working towards. So these are very, very practical, you know, the priorities here. And the reason that they're practical is that if you can succeed at these, you're pretty much ready for the next life stage. If someone is leaving home and they're away from home and they don't have life skills and they can't somehow get a job or handle money, or they are completely isolated and have no friends and no one knows them, or if they have, you know, they have a job and they have friends but they have no sense of where they're going in life, well then you can see how that can become a challenge, maybe not immediately, but at some point when they look at the next stage of life they may be saying, wow, you know, I never really tackled that. I really need to handle it now. One thing also, Magalie, the way that you look at life stages that we've been talking about is that, you know, very often people feel like they have life stages that they want to recover from the past or that they have different feelings about life stages that they want to get back, right? Yeah, and I think this comes out a lot when you talk about a person's life and their past. You might hit upon a certain stage that they went through too quickly. I have a friend that was telling me about her life story and she said that at age 21 she met an older man, he was maybe in his mid-30s, they were married very quickly, he had older children, and she went from being in her final year of college to being a mother of, I think, like an eight-year-old or something like this, and so she skipped an entire life stage of being a young woman getting a job, falling in love, and having a family on her own for the first time. She jumped into his life that was more mature. So that's a typical thing that will happen and your client may or may not see that as a negative, and you don't have to see it ever as a negative, but sometimes people need an explanation of why now they may be yearning for something that seems immature to them, or they want to have an experience that doesn't fit in with how other people around them see where they should be at. For example, sometimes people want to take a trip around the world and their friends say, but you're too old to do that. That would be a negative. Or guys in their 50s like to drive around in Ferraris and people say, you're too old. That's a whole other lecture. That's a whole other life stage. But it's a life stage issue, so you will sometimes find that people have desires that are bound with a life stage or that are connected to a life stage. So another example, obviously, is Joaquin, a late career athlete. He's 36. One of the lessons that Tony had, or the points that Tony made with him about his career, is that there are certain things that pro athletes need to do. They have their very foreshortened careers, right? I mean, they have maybe a 10-15 year career of a pro athlete. So one, they need to set aside earnings from their peak earnings years. So we all hear about athletes who spend every dime as it comes through, and then their career is over in seven to 10 or 15 years, whatever, or cut short by an injury, and then they have problems. The second one is reset their priorities and needs so that they can draw satisfaction and fulfillment from post-athletic life. This is anyone who's been a celebrity or been in an intense public situation where they get a lot of attention, is that they may, you know, they basically will move to another phase where they don't have the same levels of significance. They may not be the top of the pile, and they need to have strong priorities and needs so that they're connected to something else in their life in another way of contributing and another way of growing. And the third is a business consideration for an athlete is to capitalize on a prestige and reputation to build the new career opportunities and profit centers. So in Joaquin's case, for instance, he was working on that with his camps, right? I mean, people knew who Joaquin Hawkins was. He was a locally well-known celebrity, so he built a camp out of that. So this is just an example. Three priorities that any athlete who's in their late 30s has got to consider. And as a coach, if you understand that you're dealing with an athlete, we also do have a lot of people in our program who are athletic coaches who have that crisis happen for the athlete in their 30s, where suddenly the things that motivated them in their early 20s that drive and they're given up on having a personal life or having a family, and in the 30s they really want that and suddenly they're blaming their career. So these are life stages to consider. And life stages is really a frame of mind of realizing that there is a context to your client's desires. And if you understand something about that person's context, you can help them much more quickly and understand them much more quickly. There's also, it's important, I think, to point out that there's also life stages within relationships, within types of relationships. So as a parent, there's a life stage of having young children. There's a life stage of having school-aged children. There's a life stage of launching your children out into the world and being the parent of an adult. Then there's a life stage of being a grandparent. So within that, a coach could come up with three important goals or questions for each of those life stages for a parent. So key decisions is that you know what's really important about a key decision is that people make these decisions and they associate with them so closely. They're usually made in a situation where there's a lot of stress or a lot of pressure like you know when you make, you decide you know people suck or people are great or I'm in all this self or I'm here for contribution. It's usually in reaction to a very strong significant stimulus in the person's life. So very often highly stressful situation and people forget that they made the decision and they forget that it was made in a certain context. And then they associate with, that's just who I am. So when you're talking to someone and you sense that there may be a key decision like to say, yeah you know well I had this, I worked as a lawyer and then I said screw that. Yeah, you know, you know, an example I love of that Mark is a business owner, a business owner I know who told me that he had a very successful business with 20 employees. And he was surprised by having a manager quit. And so he decided he could no longer be a business person, sold everything and went on a tour of the world. And felt that, still felt years later that that key decision dictated who he could be today. Instead of valuing the years he had had enjoying himself as a business owner. So going back to the key decision, helping them understand the context of the key decision, helping them understand that there are parts of the decision that were maybe too much or maybe need to be reconsidered. For instance, with this man to bring back the reasons for that person's quitting, maybe he just needed to change his managerial style a little bit and he could start up that business again. Rather than having this big, emotionally charged generalization, I just can't have a business. Right. Great. So key decisions is a great way to help people recover their potential and especially when it's combined with the life stage. We have someone, for instance, who was, you know, they were in a certain business life cycle and they got frustrated with it and they gave up on business, but maybe he still had some business growth left in him that he wanted to pursue. So if you help him by understanding the key decision. And these are usually generalizations that have a lot of emotion in them. Yeah. So when people make these kind of statements that let you know it was a key decision, it's a generalization that comes with a lot of emotion. Yeah. And generalizations have a lot of emotion because they cover everything. Right. They're about everything. Right. Yeah. Great. And so the next one is finding the spark. Finding the spark is, again, an approach that we take that's really important. When you're a coach, you need to believe that whatever challenge your client is dealing with, they have the emotional resources to solve the problem. What this means is that inexperienced coaches and therapists will meet a client and they will make decisions about that client's limitations or they'll completely buy into who they are presenting in that one session. So they'll think, oh, this person's shy. Okay. Hold on. I got to shelter this person. I have to protect this person. They're shy and I'm not going to challenge them. They completely conform to the client's first presentation of themselves. When you find the spark, you believe this person's shy. I wonder if they can be funny. This person's timid. I wonder if they can be courageous. So in the finding the spark strategy, you are the investigator. You have to have that curiosity and you're looking for that spark in the person. And you'll see this in Tony constantly because he will start to speak and say something and he's making guesses. If you may not know something is true, he's watching to see if that person has a spark and he sees it as a physiological response. But in the coaching situation, you'll be looking for a moment of enthusiasm, desire, humor, interest, passion, connection, or even anger. Even when the person says screw that, that's not me or you're wrong, that will guide you to the person's true emotional resources. And so... You know, a funny... Sorry. No, go for it. So just a funny story I have about finding the spark was talking to a young man who's a teenager that presented as very dark and negative. And everything was about being intelligent and critical. But so over the top in this way that I guessed that there was a spark in there of total warmth and connection and desire to like people. And I guess that's because he was presenting too much the opposite for that not to be true. So sometimes people, especially teenagers, will present in the opposite way of who they are deep inside. So I said to this boy, you know, my guess is that inside, and I say boy, but I think he was 18, I said, I think inside you're really a golden retriever. When I said that, of course, for a moment he was shocked. And then he smiled and he wanted to talk about that. He wanted to know more. What did I mean? What was a golden retriever like if they were a person? And it opened up a whole new level of interaction and conversation to get to what he was like a golden retriever about. What was he enthusiastic about? What did he like? If he was a golden retriever, that would completely change the identity he was presenting with. So that was a fun way to find his spark as an example. And you can be very playful about it. Yeah, so you know, go ahead. Another example I thought of was a man I was coaching with relationship issues. And I took a guess and asked him if he actually had a lot of women that found him very attractive. He was presenting as just a tortured soul over a breakup and he would never get over this woman and he would never have a good relationship. So I asked him about all the women that were after him and found him attractive. And I guess I just said, and I guess you're actually very good with women, all kinds of women. And so then he had to explain to me all the ways he was actually very good with women. So this was another example of, you know, as a coach making an educated guess that he was presenting in such pain about a relationship with a woman in such a sensitive, almost feminine way, I could then make the guess that he actually had some skills with women and that there were some women out there that appreciated that in him. And so when I asked him questions about those women, he could totally shift gears. He could become very enthusiastic and see that he had the potential in the future of engaging those women if he was able to get over the woman that was breaking his heart in the moment. Great. And suddenly you're dealing with a different guy. And because people actually, they feel that spark in them and they may build a whole defensive structure around it. So they're either trying to suppress it because they don't want it to be true or it was true once but now it hasn't happened for a long time and so they don't want to engage with it. Or they feel like if someone saw how funny they were, they would think they were stupid or they were disencouraged. So you have to find that spark. People, and when you take that kind of, I have a guess about you, it's very intriguing. And even if you're 50% right, you are getting way closer to a strong kind of intimate relationship where you're really getting to the true nature of the person. And that is what builds that relationship to the coaching relationship where you can really help them to the next level. Yeah, and sometimes the way this slide tells you, sometimes it's just a shift in their tone of voice that you hear when they're talking about something like a client talking about when they were in a band as a teenager. You hear that spark in them. So you can follow up with, well, now, how do you get that same excitement? What could you do this week that will bring you more interaction with music and a social life? So they may not have abandoned the garage now, but they could go out and listen to music that they probably could never have experienced as a teenager. Great. So the next, yes, exactly. It actually leads us to our next strategy, which is the bright spot strategy. And so when the client is telling you something and you feel like they're bringing up a moment where their needs were met in a strong way sometime in the past, sometimes where they feel, you know, wow, that was great, or I love that, you know. The principle is that when you find something that works for the person, we call it a bright spot and we try to understand what made it work, which is you can recreate it or you can bring it back. So when someone reminisces about, you know, they felt more connected to certain activities or relationships or their identity or their situation, think to yourself, wow, so what if we just did more of that? That's an important ingredient in helping the person feel fully alive. And sometimes we search for it in the coaching conversation by asking a client to tell us when they felt really free, when did they feel really adventurous, if a person presents as their problem or their struggle in their life story is always being stuck or always being unsure or hesitant. Then you can ask direct questions about things they do where in that moment they feel the opposite. So often a person may have all kinds of issues in their relationships or hesitancy, but when they exercise they feel really free and alive. So that's something that just by talking about it and having them tell you a story of a moment where they felt the most that way, you're bringing up that person's understanding of their ability to experience that different side of themselves. Perfect. Right, and so our sixth strategy is mode of being. We talked about this when we talked about Joaquin Hawkins. And mode of being is an idea, what's basically the idea that there's ways that we relate to everything that kind of, if you can get a sense of that in the client, you can really help suit and understand how you can help them. And Joaquin Hawkins, with Joaquin, he was really a team player at heart. He was a team player. He thought about being on the team when he talked about all of his achievements and it was about being on the team. He didn't need to be number one on the team. He didn't need to be the captain. He didn't need to be the superstar. He wanted to be on the team. And so, when he talked about being on the team, it destroyed his identity. Probably if they had said, we can't pay you but you can be on the team for a year and you have to do something else, he would have been much better off. Being cut away from the team really hurt him because his mode of being was to be on the team. And so, Tony, understanding of this about Joaquin set him up with different kinds of teams. He set him up with a team of the family at Fiji and how the family works together. That resonated strongly with Joaquin because he understands that dynamic. He set him up with the garbage collection agents, you know, garbage collection company, where he works closely with one or two or three guys who all shoulder the kind of burden and they work together physically and they operate as a team that way. And that way, he was able to reassociate Joaquin to being more of a team with his family because he was used to being in team. Yeah, go ahead. No, I just wanted to touch on how that can work with your clients who have different modes of being other than Joaquin. So, for example, if you help a person understand that they're really happy when they are able to just observe, then you can start to think of a list of ways during their course of their week or their day. That they can take moments where they're getting just to observe. So, let's say they have to be a leader at work. They don't have a choice. They've been given, they've been elevated into that position. But what they really love to do is to observe. Then you could talk to them about how good leaders understand everyone inside of their company or their workplace. So, you can give them homework to do, which is in sync with their strengths. And so, by observing, they become better at their job. By using their desire to observe, they think of dates they can take their wife or girlfriend or their husband or boyfriend or friends out on where they get to do what they love. And in this way, they become more popular or more likable because they're not going against their nature. Instead, they're sharing a strength with other people, like we can sit and people watch at a cafe. And the observer person can have all kinds of interesting things to say about when they're watching people that they couldn't have if they were put in the position of being a critic or a philosopher. So, just an example. Yeah, and the other thing that's important about mode of being is helping people to embrace their nature, the type of mode of being that they go into naturally. So, some people, for instance, are extremely detail-oriented, right? And they sense every, pick up on every detail and they track a lot of things. And then, you know, maybe someone tells them, hey, you're wrong or that's bad or you're being whatever. And they have to realize, you know what, this is, you know, as a coach, you could say, this is one of your modes of being. You pick up on detail. You do this. And you can help people affirm that rather than feeling like something is wrong or causing conflict. Absolutely. Okay, so that's the six strategies that we use that are really useful in setting up a life story type of interview with a client. The reason I do this is to understand as much as I can about you, the important events from your life, the people who were the key players in your life. So, that can be said in several sentences over time to get people to... Yeah. But, you know, I think that is a good overview because sometimes people need to understand why they're providing you with this information and what you're going to do with it. And especially, by the way, this is a kind of trade secret, is when people invest their story with you, they've invested a lot with you as a coach. There's not that many people that they've had the chance to be able to share in this, you know, with no other agenda on the table about their lives. So, it's a great way to get the client to feel like you know them, that you understand them. It's really good for the relationship, you know, as the coach-client relationship. So, let's talk about some questions that you may want to ask. So, this is just a list of questions that you can start off with. And, of course, grow your own questions as a coach. But some of them are, tell me a little about your family growing up. Do you have siblings? Where did you live? What was your home like? Would you say your younger years had a theme? Was there a person in your life, a friend or family member who gave you a lot of wisdom, magic, or peace? That's a question that we often use if a person's had a lot of hardship or neglect or feelings of being a victim. We then help our clients find someone from their past who gave them a sense of happiness or peace or wisdom. It could be a neighbor, a teacher, anyone, a friend's parent. But we help the client find one person that they think of as a fairy godmother or godfather that was there for them that they can associate with and they can draw strength from now today, even if their parents weren't that good to them or life was hard for them. Great. So another question is any major decisions of a kid that you remember making? That draws, that takes you into the key decisions when you ask a question like that. Well, that's really important information they've given you of how you could view it as, well, they let go of something they loved, or you could also view it as that they had the strength, you're elevating them now, to see it that they had the strength to shift gears and do something else. That's very typical of them. I'm saying you're elevating them right there, and so you've established the resource that they have. And that's something that I typically find in clients who have a lot of insecurities is they have that kind of story about their life, that they didn't do what they thought they would do, and they're weak or dumber or less motivated or successful because they changed gears. And they really need a reframing of that for themselves to an empowering point where they see that, you know, many people would have taken a totally different route down when they realized they couldn't do something, but instead they just shifted into something they could do and how that's a really empowering thing to be able to do in life. And you could talk about, you can bring it into the present then, how could they use that same strength to shift gears right now with whatever presenting challenge they're talking to you about or whatever goal they have.
